title: Contend for the Faith
subtitle: Warnings against false teachers, examples of judgment, and the doxology of assurance

heb.paragraph: Kept for Jesus Christ - the recipients are guarded by divine power. Their security is not their own achievement but God preservation.
Contend for the faith once for all delivered to the saints - the faith is a fixed deposit requiring vigorous defense.

heb.gloss: to slip in secretly, sneak in
heb.paragraph: Certain people have crept in unnoticed - the false teachers infiltrated the community. Their entry was covert, their intentions hostile.

refs.note: False teachers will secretly bring in destructive heresies.
False brothers secretly brought in to spy out our freedom.
Guard the deposit entrusted to you.
Hold firm to the trustworthy word as taught.

notes.note: Jude, servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James, to those called, beloved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ.
I had to write urging you to contend for the faith once delivered. Certain people have crept in, perverting grace into sensuality, denying our Master.
Calvin notes that Jude does not claim apostleship but servant status. His authority rests on his message, not his office.
The faith once delivered is a fixed body of truth. Innovation in doctrine is not progress but perversion.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Brother of James identifies Jude as a brother of Jesus (Matthew 13:55). He does not claim this relationship but emphasizes servanthood.
Long ago designated (palai progegrammenoi) suggests divine foreknowledge of the false teachers judgment.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that contending for the faith is not optional but urgent. The faith is objective truth requiring defense.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes the triad of security: called, beloved, kept. Election, love, and preservation secure the readers against false teaching.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude/2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green emphasizes that the false teachers turn grace into license. They abuse freedom to justify immorality.

hist.historical: Jude, identifying himself as 'brother of James' (and thus brother of Jesus, Mark 6:3), writes urgently against false teachers who have 'crept in unnoticed' (v. 4). These opponents pervert grace into license for immorality and 'deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ.' Jude draws extensively on Jewish apocalyptic tradition, citing 1 Enoch (vv. 14-15) and the Assumption of Moses (v. 9) — texts familiar to his Jewish-Christian audience though not part of the Hebrew Bible. The letter's three Old Testament examples (Israel in the wilderness, fallen angels, Sodom and Gomorrah) and three 'woe' figures (Cain, Balaam, Korah) create a rhetorical pattern of divine judgment against apostasy. The striking parallels with 2 Peter 2 suggest literary dependence.
hist.context: Jude, servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James, writes to those called, beloved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ. Though he wanted to write about common salvation, necessity compels him to address false teachers who have crept in unnoticed - people long ago designated for condemnation, ungodly persons who pervert grace into sensuality and deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ.

heb.paragraph: Angels who did not stay within their own position but left their proper dwelling - rebellion against assigned order brings judgment.
Woe to them! For they walked in the way of Cain, abandoned themselves to Balaam error, perished in Korah rebellion.
Wandering stars, for whom the gloom of utter darkness has been reserved forever - they follow no fixed course, destined for blackness.

refs.note: Israel destroyed in the wilderness for unbelief.
Sons of God and daughters of men - angelic sin.
The Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones.

notes.note: Three examples: Israel destroyed after deliverance, angels in chains for leaving their position, Sodom destroyed for sexual immorality.
These people defile flesh, reject authority, blaspheme glorious ones. Michael did not dare pronounce judgment on Satan but said The Lord rebuke you.
Woe! Way of Cain, error of Balaam, rebellion of Korah. Hidden reefs, waterless clouds, fruitless trees, wild waves, wandering stars.
Enoch prophesied: The Lord comes with myriads to execute judgment. They are grumblers, malcontents, following their own desires, loud-mouthed boasters, flattering people.
Calvin notes the escalating examples: people, angels, cities. None escapes judgment. Privilege does not protect from consequences.
Michael restraint contrasts with the false teachers arrogance. Even an archangel defers judgment to God.
Calvin acknowledges that Enoch quotation is not from canonical Scripture but treats it as true prophecy preserved in tradition.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The angels who left their proper dwelling are likely the Watchers tradition from 1 Enoch - angels who had illicit relations with women.
Michael disputing about Moses body comes from the Assumption of Moses, a Jewish pseudepigraphal work. Jude uses it illustratively.
The Enoch quotation is from 1 Enoch 1:9. Jude quotes it as prophecy without necessarily endorsing the entire book as Scripture.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes the rhetorical force: three examples from salvation history, three woes from Israel apostasy, seven descriptive images of corruption.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids emphasizes that Jude draws on Jewish interpretive tradition (1 Enoch, Assumption of Moses). His audience knew these texts.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude/2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green notes the vivid imagery: hidden reefs, waterless clouds, autumn trees without fruit, wild waves, wandering stars. Each image conveys emptiness and danger.

hist.context: Jude reminds readers of three OT examples: Israel saved from Egypt but later destroyed for unbelief; angels who abandoned their position kept in chains for judgment; Sodom and Gomorrah destroyed for sexual immorality. These people similarly defile the flesh, reject authority, and blaspheme the glorious ones. Michael the archangel, disputing about Moses body, did not dare pronounce a blasphemous judgment but said The Lord rebuke you. But these people blaspheme what they do not understand. Woe to them! They walk in Cain way, rush into Balaam error, perish in Korah rebellion. They are hidden reefs at your love feasts, waterless clouds, fruitless trees twice dead, wild waves foaming up shame, wandering stars reserved for utter darkness. Enoch prophesied: The Lord comes with ten thousands of his holy ones to execute judgment on all.

sections.header: Verses 17-23 - "Remember the Apostles; Rescue the Wavering"

heb.paragraph: Scoffers following their own ungodly passions - the apostles predicted such people would come in the last time.
Worldly people, devoid of the Spirit - they operate on merely human level, lacking the Spirit who enables spiritual discernment.
Save others by snatching them out of the fire - urgent rescue of those endangered by false teaching.

refs.note: Scoffers will come in the last days, following their own sinful desires.
The natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit.
In later times some will depart from the faith.
A brand plucked from the fire.

notes.note: Remember the apostles predictions: scoffers will come following ungodly passions. These cause divisions, worldly, devoid of the Spirit.
Build yourselves up in most holy faith, pray in the Holy Spirit, keep yourselves in God love, wait for the mercy of our Lord.
Have mercy on doubters. Save others by snatching them from fire. Show mercy with fear, hating the garment stained by flesh.
Calvin notes that the apostolic predictions included warnings about internal enemies. The church was forewarned.
Building up, praying, keeping, waiting - four activities characterize those who will persevere against false teaching.
Three different responses to three different situations: mercy to doubters, urgent rescue for the endangered, cautious mercy for the contaminated.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Devoid of the Spirit (pneuma me echontes) - the false teachers lack the Holy Spirit. Their worldliness reveals their spiritual bankruptcy.
The text-critical situation is complex with variant readings. Some manuscripts have two groups, others three. The meaning is pastoral discernment.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes the balance: build yourselves up (positive) while rescuing the endangered (outward). Believers must tend both to themselves and to others.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes the triad of pastoral response: some need gentle mercy, some need urgent rescue, some need cautious engagement. One size does not fit all.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude/2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green emphasizes that the warning is not to abandon the wandering but to rescue them wisely. Compassion and caution must combine.

hist.context: But remember the predictions of the apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ - they told you that in the last time there would be scoffers following their own ungodly passions. These are the people who cause divisions, worldly people, devoid of the Spirit. But you, beloved, building yourselves up in your most holy faith, praying in the Holy Spirit, keep yourselves in the love of God, waiting for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ that leads to eternal life. Have mercy on those who doubt; save others by snatching them from fire; to others show mercy with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh.

sections.header: Verses 24-25 - "To Him Who Is Able to Keep You"

heb.paragraph: To him who is able to keep you from stumbling - divine preservation is the ground of assurance. God guards his people.
Keep you from stumbling - the term denotes sure-footed stability. God enables believers to stand firm.
Majesty, dominion, and authority - the doxology ascribes to God supreme greatness, sovereign rule, and ultimate power.

refs.note: To him who is able to strengthen you... to the only wise God be glory forever.
The God of peace will sanctify you completely... He who calls you is faithful.
To him who is able to do far more abundantly than all we ask or think.
He who began a good work in you will bring it to completion.

notes.note: To him who is able to keep you from stumbling and present you blameless with great joy - to the only God our Savior through Jesus Christ be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority forever.
Calvin notes that the doxology answers the letter problem: false teachers threaten; God keeps. Divine power is the antidote to human frailty.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase before the presence of his glory envisions the eschatological presentation of believers to God - the culmination of salvation.
Before all time and now and forever - the threefold time reference emphasizes God eternal sovereignty.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the doxology grounds assurance in God ability, not human effort. The same God who calls also keeps.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes that the doxology is one of the most magnificent in the NT. It completes the letter by returning from warning to worship.

green.source: Gene Green, BECNT Jude/2 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Green emphasizes the pastoral comfort: after all the warnings about false teachers, Jude assures readers that God is able to keep them.

hist.context: Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy - to the only God, our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time and now and forever. Amen.

rows.label: vv. 5-16
rows.text: Examples of judgment; woes; imagery of corruption

rows.label: vv. 17-23
rows.text: Apostolic warning; build up; rescue the wavering

rows.label: vv. 24-25
rows.text: Doxology: to him who is able to keep you

lit.note: Jude is a single chapter of urgent warning and magnificent assurance. False teachers threaten, but God keeps his people.

themes.note: Judgment dominates through the OT examples. Sovereignty is strong in the keeping doxology. Faith must be defended. Mercy characterizes the response to the wavering.

coaching.tip: Jude planned a different letter — 'I was very eager to write to you about our common salvation' — but changed course because of crisis: 'certain people have crept in unnoticed' (vv. 3-4). Pastoral letters often respond to immediate situations. The doctrine Jude teaches is shaped by the error he confronts.

Jude draws examples from Israel's wilderness generation (v. 5), fallen angels (v. 6), and Sodom (v. 7). Each rejected God's assigned place: Israel refused the promised land, angels left their dwelling, Sodom pursued unnatural desire. The pattern: rebellion against God's order brings judgment. The false teachers fit this trajectory.

References to 1 Enoch (v. 14) and possibly the Assumption of Moses (v. 9) are jarring — Jude quotes non-canonical texts as authoritative. This doesn't canonize those books but shows Jewish literature shaped early Christian imagination. Jude's readers knew these texts. Inspiration didn't require isolation from culture.

The doxology (vv. 24-25) — 'to him who is able to keep you from stumbling' — answers the anxiety the letter might create. Jude has warned of apostasy, false teachers, judgment. But God keeps his people. The letter ends not with fear but confidence: God's ability exceeds every threat. Pastoral warnings need pastoral comfort.
